data.title: Diageo (LSE:DGE) Stock Looks Cheap Despite A 51% Three Year Slide
data.description:
Diageo’s share price has had a tough run over the last few years, yet its current valuation checks suggest the stock now screens as relatively cheap on several measures, creating a clear tension between weak past returns and a more supportive valuation profile.

  • Over the past 3 years, Diageo’s share price has declined about 50.5%, which puts recent returns and sentiment firmly on the back foot.
  • For a global branded spirits group like Diageo, long term brand strength and cash generation can support valuation, while any sustained pressure on volumes, pricing, or input costs may weigh on what investors are willing to pay.
  • Across Simply Wall St’s broader checks, Diageo screens as undervalued in 6 of 6 valuation tests, which leans toward the stock being priced below what those fundamentals imply.

The issue now is whether Diageo’s current share price already reflects the weak past share performance or if the valuation signals indicate room for a more favorable outcome ahead.

Find out why Diageo's -18.2% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Is Diageo Still Cheap on Earnings?

The P/E ratio suits Diageo because earnings are a key way investors weigh the value of established consumer brands against what they are paying for each share.

Right now, Diageo trades on a P/E of about 18.9x, which sits slightly above the Beverage industry average of roughly 17.4x but far below the peer group average of about 43.3x. Simply Wall St’s fair P/E ratio for Diageo is 22.9x, reflecting what the multiple might be given its business profile, margins and risk level. Against that yardstick, the current P/E is several turns lower, indicating that investors are not paying as much for each pound of earnings as this framework would imply.

For you as a shareholder or prospective buyer, this gap between the current 18.9x and the fair ratio of 22.9x highlights a stock that appears on the cheaper side relative to the earnings multiple suggested by these fundamentals.

On the P/E multiple alone, Diageo stock currently appears undervalued relative to the earnings-based fair ratio.

For Diageo, Simply Wall St Narratives sit between the current valuation puzzle and the underlying expectations, spelling out what would need to happen to growth, margins and earnings for the stock to be worth materially more, or less, than today’s price on the Community page. Each one sets out Diageo’s implied fair value as a thesis about how the business might develop so you can see over time whether that view holds up.

Community views on Diageo pull in very different directions, depending on whether you focus on future cash generation or on structural headwinds to volumes and margins.

The Bottom Line

data.title: DuPont (DD) Stock May Trade At A Discount Despite Lithium Extraction Launch
data.description:
DuPont de Nemours has delivered a 59.9% return over the past five years, yet its valuation checks are split, with the Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) intrinsic value pointing to meaningful upside while traditional market multiples suggest the stock screens as expensive.

  • Over five years, DuPont de Nemours has returned 59.9%. This puts current investors squarely in the camp of asking how much of the story is already reflected in the price.
  • Growth expectations around DuPont de Nemours' roles in personal protective equipment and direct lithium extraction can support optimism on future cash flows. At the same time, ongoing legal challenges linked to PFAS lawsuits may weigh on how much of that potential investors are willing to price in today.
  • The company scores 3 out of 6 on our valuation checks, which points to a mixed picture rather than a clear bargain or clear overvaluation.

The issue now is whether DuPont de Nemours' share price at US$137.40 offers enough margin of safety when the DCF suggests it is undervalued by 36.3% but earnings based multiples lean rich.

Find out why DuPont de Nemours' 49.1% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Is DuPont de Nemours a Bargain on Cash Flow?

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) approach estimates what DuPont de Nemours is worth based on the cash it can generate for shareholders over time. For DuPont de Nemours, the model starts from latest twelve month free cash flow of about $892.3 million and assumes that cash flows grow rather than shrink, but at relatively moderate rates instead of aggressive step changes.

On these assumptions, the DCF points to an intrinsic value of about $215.79 per share, compared with the current share price of $137.40, implying the stock screens as 36.3% undervalued. Recent headlines around DuPont launching an end to end direct lithium extraction portfolio help explain why some investors may see room for those cash flows to hold up, even as ongoing PFAS legal issues remain in focus.

On the DCF numbers alone, DuPont de Nemours looks undervalued relative to the cash flows analysts expect it to produce.

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests DuPont de Nemours is undervalued by 36.3%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 38 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for DuPont de Nemours.

Does DuPont de Nemours Look Pricey on Earnings?

The P/E ratio is a useful way to think about what you are paying today for each dollar of DuPont de Nemours' earnings. On this measure, DuPont de Nemours trades on about 140.5x earnings, far above both the Chemicals industry average of 25.2x and a peer group average of 20.8x.

A more tailored fair P/E multiple for DuPont de Nemours, based on factors such as its growth profile, margins, size and risk, is around 29.2x. The fact that the current P/E is several times higher than this suggests the market is pricing in a lot of optimism and is heavily discounting risks like the ongoing PFAS legal issues. The model is effectively signalling that, on earnings, the stock screens as very expensive rather than pointing to a precise fair value target.

On the P/E multiple, DuPont de Nemours currently looks overvalued compared with both its sector and what the tailored model suggests would be a more grounded earnings multiple.

The DuPont de Nemours Narrative: What Would Justify Today's Price?

Simply Wall St Narratives pick up where this DuPont de Nemours valuation puzzle leaves off. They spell out the specific growth, margin and earnings paths that would need to play out for the stock to be worth materially more or less than its current price on the market. Each one links its number to a clear view on how growth, profitability and risks could evolve so you can revisit those assumptions on the Community page as fresh information comes through.

The DuPont de Nemours community is split, with one camp seeing significant upside potential and the other focused on long running structural risks.

Bull case: 28% undervalued

"DuPont's leadership in advanced materials for EVs, lightweighting, and electrification, combined with a sharpened R&D focus post-spin, positions the company to capture disproportionate share of the rapid global shift toward electric transportation..."

Read the full Bull Case to see why DuPont de Nemours could be undervalued

Bear case: 175% overvalued

"Intensifying global ESG and environmental regulations are poised to increase DuPont's long-term compliance and remediation costs, especially as ongoing PFAS and other environmental liabilities continue to create significant legal and cash flow burdens over the next decades, which will erode net margins and suppress future earnings growth..."

The Bottom Line

For DuPont de Nemours, the Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) intrinsic value points to meaningful upside, while the earnings based view flags the stock as overvalued on a very stretched P/E multiple. That split reflects a market willing to pay a high price for earnings today, and a cash flow model that assumes DuPont can support those cash flows over time. data.title: Apple (AAPL) Faces Samsung’s New Foldables As Foldable iPhone Expectations Build
data.description:
• Samsung has launched three new foldable smartphones, adding fresh competition in the premium device segment.
  • The launches come as Apple, ticker NasdaqGS:AAPL, is reported to be preparing its own foldable device entry.
  • The timing sets up a direct comparison between Samsung's current foldable line up and expectations for a potential foldable iPhone.

For investors watching Apple, the latest move from Samsung focuses attention on how a future foldable iPhone could fit into Apple's broader product line. Apple shares recently closed at $321.66, with the stock up 18.7% year to date and 51.1% over the past year. Returns of 68.8% over three years and 126.6% over five years show how closely the market tends to track shifts in Apple's hardware cycle.

The expectation that Apple may capture significant share of the foldable smartphone segment adds weight to how and when the company enters this category. Samsung's new devices effectively set a benchmark for pricing, features, and user experience that Apple will be measured against. For shareholders, the key question is how a foldable product might influence Apple's future device mix and longer term revenue profile.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Apple by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Apple.

Does the team leading Apple have what it takes? See our full breakdown of the management team's track record and compensation.

For Apple, the timing of Samsung’s three new foldable phones puts John Ternus’ early product decisions under the spotlight. Ternus has been closely linked to Apple’s hardware roadmap, so investors will be watching how a future foldable iPhone reflects his approach to balancing design, durability, and integration with Apple Intelligence and services. Samsung, Huawei and other Android handset makers already have multiple foldable generations in market, so Apple’s leadership team is effectively choosing to arrive later with a product that will be judged against a defined set of features, form factors and price points.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Apple Narrative

  • The expected foldable iPhone supports the existing Apple narrative that new device formats, paired with AI-powered features and a growing services ecosystem, can keep users inside the Apple hardware and software loop for longer.
  • Samsung’s head start in foldables, together with strong efforts from Huawei and Xiaomi, challenges the assumption that Apple can rely solely on ecosystem strength without matching or exceeding competitors’ hardware experiments.
  • The narrative around Apple focuses heavily on AI assistants, services and emerging markets, but it does not fully address how leadership under John Ternus might handle higher hardware complexity, repairability and long-term support for foldable devices.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Apple to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ A misstep on foldable hardware under new leadership, whether through reliability issues or a design that fails to stand out against Samsung and Huawei, could raise questions about Apple’s product-execution track record.
  • ⚠️ Entering the foldable category at premium pricing without clear differentiation could compress unit volumes or push customers toward cheaper Android alternatives, especially if repair costs or durability concerns surface.
  • 🎁 A carefully timed foldable launch that integrates tightly with Apple Intelligence, services and wearables could reinforce Apple’s ecosystem and give Ternus early credibility as a product-focused CEO.
  • 🎁 If Apple uses its late entry to address pain points seen in early foldables, such as hinge reliability and screen longevity, it could strengthen perceptions of quality and help sustain high-end iPhone demand.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, watch how Apple under John Ternus talks about foldables on earnings calls and product events, especially any comments on where a foldable iPhone would sit in the existing line up. Pay attention to how quickly Samsung iterates on its new Galaxy Z Fold and Z Flip models, and whether Apple references those devices when framing its own choices. It is also worth tracking investor and user reaction to early leaks or prototypes, as this will offer clues on whether Apple is prioritizing ultra-premium pricing, mass-market appeal, or a gradual, lower-volume introduction into the foldable category.

data.title: Nokia (HLSE:NOKIA) Expands Into Defense With AI Command Tools For Deployable 5G
data.description:
• Nokia Oyj (HLSE:NOKIA) and NestAI have delivered AI enabled command and control capabilities for deployable 5G networks.
  • The collaboration targets defense users that require secure, portable 5G connectivity in challenging field conditions.
  • The development extends Nokia's activity beyond traditional telecom use cases into defense oriented applications of 5G and AI.

Nokia Oyj is widely associated with network infrastructure and 5G equipment, and this new work with NestAI shows the company applying its technology in defense settings. Deployable 5G networks with AI driven command and control sit at the intersection of secure communications and complex field operations, an area that many telecom peers have not focused on. For investors following HLSE:NOKIA, it introduces a different use case than recent AI RAN and commercial 5G deployment news.

For readers tracking longer term themes, the move highlights how 5G and AI tools can be configured for critical communications in difficult environments, not just for consumer data traffic. This type of defense focused connectivity may influence how Nokia allocates resources across product lines and partnerships, and it could shape which customers and contracts become more important over time.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Nokia Oyj by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Nokia Oyj.

2 things going right for Nokia Oyj that this headline doesn't cover.

For Nokia Oyj, the NestAI collaboration adds another AI-focused product to sit alongside its AI-RAN platform and recent 5G work with Taiwan Mobile. The new command-and-control tools are tailored for deployable 5G networks in contested environments, which points to a customer set and buying cycle very different from commercial operators. For investors, this sits next to Q2 2026 results that showed €4,815 million in quarterly sales and net income of €2 million, as well as guidance that assumes a 3% to 7% quarter-on-quarter net sales increase for Q3 2026. The defense-focused solution does not change those figures. However, it gives more detail on how Nokia is trying to widen the set of 5G and AI use cases that could support future contracts, including outside traditional carrier spending where companies such as Ericsson and Huawei are strongest.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Nokia Oyj Narrative

  • The move into AI-enabled defense command and control fits with the narrative of Nokia targeting higher-margin, IP-heavy network solutions tied to AI and advanced 5G and 6G use cases.
  • Relying on specialized defense deployments could test execution, because timing, certification and procurement processes differ from the cloud and carrier demand that the narrative highlights.
  • The narrative focuses heavily on optical, cloud and hyperscaler demand, and may not fully reflect the potential role of mission-critical defense contracts in Nokia Oyj's longer-term revenue mix.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Nokia Oyj to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Nokia Oyj has a highly volatile share price over the past 3 months compared to the Finnish market, which can make short-term returns unpredictable around new product announcements.
  • ⚠️ Recent results include large one-off items and profit margins of 3.9%, which are lower than last year, so investors may want to see clearer margin trends as new AI and defense offerings scale.
  • 🎁 Earnings are forecast to grow 22.21% per year, which gives context for why Nokia is investing in AI-powered networks and new segments like defense-focused 5G command and control.
  • 🎁 The stock is currently trading at 24.3% below one estimate of its fair value, which some investors may view as room for future contracts and AI-related products to be reflected in the price.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it is worth watching how often Nokia Oyj converts this type of AI-enabled defense solution into repeat contracts and multi-country deployments, and whether those wins show up in segment disclosures around margins and order intake. Investors may also want to track how Nokia balances resources between carrier-focused AI-RAN offerings and more specialized defense projects, as well as how its position compares with Ericsson and Huawei in secure 5G and AI control systems. The upcoming Q3 2026 results, set against the 3% to 7% net sales growth assumption, will be one reference point for whether new AI and defense products start to coincide with steadier profitability.

data.title: The Brink's Company (NYSE:BCO) Looks Like A Good Stock, And It's Going Ex-Dividend Soon
data.description:
Regular readers will know that we love our dividends at Simply Wall St, which is why it's exciting to see The Brink's Company (NYSE:BCO) is about to trade ex-dividend in the next 2 days. The ex-dividend date is one business day before the record date, which is the cut-off date for shareholders to be present on the company's books to be eligible for a dividend payment. It is important to be aware of the ex-dividend date because any trade on the stock needs to have been settled on or before the record date. Meaning, you will need to purchase Brink's' shares before the 27th of July to receive the dividend, which will be paid on the 1st of September.

The company's upcoming dividend is US$0.255 a share, following on from the last 12 months, when the company distributed a total of US$1.02 per share to shareholders. Last year's total dividend payments show that Brink's has a trailing yield of 0.8% on the current share price of US$121.09. We love seeing companies pay a dividend, but it's also important to be sure that laying the golden eggs isn't going to kill our golden goose! So we need to investigate whether Brink's can afford its dividend, and if the dividend could grow.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Dividends are usually paid out of company profits, so if a company pays out more than it earned then its dividend is usually at greater risk of being cut. Brink's paid out just 24% of its profit last year, which we think is conservatively low and leaves plenty of margin for unexpected circumstances. Yet cash flow is typically more important than profit for assessing dividend sustainability, so we should always check if the company generated enough cash to afford its dividend. Luckily it paid out just 7.8% of its free cash flow last year.

It's positive to see that Brink's's dividend is covered by both profits and cash flow, since this is generally a sign that the dividend is sustainable, and a lower payout ratio usually suggests a greater margin of safety before the dividend gets cut.

View our latest analysis for Brink's

Click here to see the company's payout ratio, plus analyst estimates of its future dividends.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Stocks in companies that generate sustainable earnings growth often make the best dividend prospects, as it is easier to lift the dividend when earnings are rising. If earnings decline and the company is forced to cut its dividend, investors could watch the value of their investment go up in smoke. That's why it's comforting to see Brink's's earnings have been skyrocketing, up 67% per annum for the past five years. Brink's looks like a real growth company, with earnings per share growing at a cracking pace and the company reinvesting most of its profits in the business.

Many investors will assess a company's dividend performance by evaluating how much the dividend payments have changed over time. Since the start of our data, 10 years ago, Brink's has lifted its dividend by approximately 9.8% a year on average. It's encouraging to see the company lifting dividends while earnings are growing, suggesting at least some corporate interest in rewarding shareholders.

To Sum It Up

Is Brink's worth buying for its dividend? Brink's has been growing earnings at a rapid rate, and has a conservatively low payout ratio, implying that it is reinvesting heavily in its business; a sterling combination. There's a lot to like about Brink's, and we would prioritise taking a closer look at it.
